_purpose: Ethnic heritage profile lookup for Layer 5. Given identification clues from an ancestor's records (surname, denomination, settlement location, occupation), Polsia finds matching heritage profile(s) to enrich the narrative with culturally-specific context.
_usage: 1. Check surname_patterns, denomination_signals, settlement_signals, occupation_signals, and record_signals in each profile entry. 2. Load matching profile(s) from ethnic_heritage/{profile_id}.json. 3. An ancestor may match MULTIPLE profiles (e.g., scots_irish + quaker, or enslaved_african_american + reconstruction_jim_crow + great_migration_african_american for a multi-generational line). 4. Use nel_cross_references to load related Layer 3/4 files. 5. Profiles with has_dignity_mandate=true require careful, dignified narration per the profile's dignity_mandate section.

surname_patterns: Core Acadian surnames with remarkable genealogical continuity: Broussard, Thibodaux/Thibodeaux, LeBlanc, Landry, Hébert/Hebert, Mouton, Guidry, Boudreaux, Trahan, Arceneaux, Babineau/Babineaux, Comeaux, Dugas, Doucet, Melancon/Melanson
Dit names (name-within-a-name): a single family may carry both a legal surname and a dit name — e.g., Landry dit Bellaire, Broussard dit Beausoleil — complicating record linkage
Prefix patterns: Le/La/De/Des (LeBlanc, LaFleur, Deshotel, DesClouds) indicate French origin
Stable patronymic pool: the same ~80 surnames recur across virtually all Acadian communities from Nova Scotia to Louisiana, reflecting the founder population bottleneck
Variant spellings across jurisdictions: Thibodaux/Thibodeaux/Thibodo, Hébert/Hebert/Abare, Boudreaux/Boudreau/Boudrot
denomination_signals: Roman Catholic (near-universal — Catholicism was central to Acadian identity and a primary reason for British hostility)
Diocese of Lafayette (est. 1918) parish records
Archdiocese of New Orleans sacramental registers (pre-1918)
Pre-expulsion: Catholic parish records from Grand-Pré, Beaubassin, Port-Royal, Pisiquid

Maine borderlands (Aroostook County — Acadian communities persisting from pre-expulsion era)

A small pool of surnames — approximately 100 surnames account for nearly all Chinese Americans before 1943. The five most common (Wong, Lee, Chan, Fong, Chin) cover a disproportionate share of the population. This surname concentration creates enormous genealogical confusion.

Baptist mission churches
Catholic missions (less common but present in San Francisco)
Buddhist and Daoist temples (Tin How Temple, San Francisco, founded 1852 — oldest Chinese temple in the US)
No denominational affiliation — many Chinese immigrants maintained traditional ancestor veneration and folk religion without formal institutional membership

surname_patterns: Post-emancipation surname adoption (1865-1870): many freedpeople chose surnames at this moment — some took the slaveholder's surname, some chose a previous slaveholder's name, some adopted names of respected figures (Washington, Lincoln, Grant), Biblical names, or trade-based names (Carpenter, Smith, Mason)
Common surnames in this population: Washington, Jefferson, Jackson, Johnson, Williams, Brown, Freeman, Grant, Lincoln, Davis (ironic adoption), Robinson, Taylor, Harris, Walker, Green, Thomas
Surnames sometimes reflecting place of origin: county names, river names, plantation names
